name: Yasakani no Magatama Jewel
description: The Yasakani no Magatama is one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, symbolizing benevolence. It is a comma-shaped jewel, traditionally made of jade, though other materials like agate or jasper may have been used. Its origins are shrouded in mystery, with some accounts attributing its creation to the deity Ame no Koyane. The magatama played a crucial role in the myth of Amaterasu's emergence from the Ama-no-Iwato cave. When the sun goddess hid herself, plunging the world into darkness, the other deities crafted the Yasakani no Magatama, along with the Yata no Kagami mirror and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword, to lure her out. The jewel, along with the mirror, was hung from a sacred sakaki tree, and its brilliance, combined with the other deities' revelry, piqued Amaterasu's curiosity. This trickery eventually led to her re-emergence, restoring light to the world. In modern times, the Yasakani no Magatama, along with the other Imperial Regalia, represents the legitimacy and authority of the Japanese Emperor. It is said to be housed at the Imperial Palace in Tokyo, though it is rarely seen by the public. The jewel is believed to possess protective powers and is a symbol of peace and prosperity. Its comma shape is often interpreted as representing the soul or the life force, and its smooth surface signifies purity and virtue. The Yasakani no Magatama remains a potent symbol of Japanese culture and heritage.
subtitle: Benevolence and Imperial Authority
origin: Crafted by deities to lure Amaterasu from cave

keyMyths.title: Amaterasu and the Cave
keyMyths.description: The Yasakani no Magatama was created along with the Yata no Kagami to entice Amaterasu out of the Ama-no-Iwato cave, restoring light to the world. It was hung on a sacred sakaki tree.

keyMyths.title: Imperial Regalia
keyMyths.description: The Yasakani no Magatama is one of the Three Sacred Treasures, symbolizing the legitimacy of the Japanese Emperor alongside the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword and the Yata no Kagami mirror.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation and annotation of the Kojiki, a key source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation and annotation of the Kojiki, a key source for Japanese mythology.
